policy_statement: Promote capital improvements in buildings to endure grid failures and facilitate safe re-energization (NYSERDA).
mechanism_description: NYSERDA should promote building capital improvements so buildings can endure grid failures and more safely accept power when the electric system is re-energized.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard/stressor (grid outage, extreme events) → building hardening/operational resilience → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ outage impacts (health/safety/economic losses)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Building capital upgrades and resilience features that allow the building to better tolerate loss of grid power and safely resume operation when power returns (e.g., electrical system hardening, controls, islanding-readiness/transfer equipment, critical load panels, surge protection, safe restart procedures).  
(2) Mediating process: Improved physical robustness and electrical safety reduces failure modes during outages and prevents equipment damage or unsafe conditions during re-energization; enhanced ability to maintain critical services reduces cascading impacts.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower vulnerability and consequences from climate-driven or climate-exacerbated outages (reduced occupant risk, reduced downtime, reduced damages and disruption).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing damage, safety risks, and service disruption to occupants during climate-related power outages by increasing building resilience to grid failure.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: addresses a climate stressor (outage risk) by reducing vulnerability through building resilience upgrades, yielding reduced outage impacts—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while some upgrades could incidentally affect energy use or emissions (e.g., efficiency, storage), the stated intent and main effect is maintaining safety and function during grid failure and re-energization, not GHG mitigation or resource efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: if implemented via on-site generation/storage, there may be mitigation co-benefits (or emissions increases if diesel backup), but these are secondary to the resilience/outage-impact objective and vary by technology choice.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy whose core mechanism is “building resilience upgrades → reduced outage impacts” is fundamentally about coping with disruptions rather than reducing emissions, so Adaptation remains primary across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., whether it’s energy resilience, critical facilities, microgrids, or codes vs. incentives) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may use fossil backup generators that increase emissions; others may use storage/microgrids that reduce emissions. These affect secondary co-benefits/harms but do not change the primary adaptation classification for the stated mechanism.
key_indicators: "promote capital improvements"; "in consultation with"; "endure grid failures"; "facilitate buildings' ability to accept power when the system is re-energized"
co_benefits: Improved electrical safety and reliability; potential energy efficiency/modernization benefits if upgrades include controls/equipment refresh.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Promote” is ambiguous between incentives, technical assistance, and guidance; classified as incentive-program because it targets capital improvements (implying funding/financing support) and is not framed as a requirement or standard.
